summary.brief: Richter v. Presbyterian Healthcare Services. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Kathryn Richter died in 2005 when she developed a heart arrhythmia during surgery intended to remove a tumor from her adrenal gland.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The heart arrhythmia was caused by an undiagnosed condition called pheochromocytoma.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the course of a hospitalization in 2001, Mrs. Richter’s then physicians ordered laboratory tests which were diagnostic of the condition that caused her death.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The tests were never read or acted on by her physicians.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm the partial directed verdict in favor of Dr. Winterkorn primarily because Plaintiff did not present sufficient expert testimony to avoid it.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Richter was admitted to PHS in April 2001 for treatment and testing related to cardiac symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Seligman, one of Mrs. Richter’s physicians, ordered catecholamine and metanephrine testing on her urine to determine whether she might have an undiagnosed pheochromocytoma.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: TriCore was responsible for processing the results of Mrs. Richter’s urine catecholamine and metanephrine testing and delivering the Lab Results to PHS.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The first test showed catecholamine levels 150 times higher than normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The second test showed catecholamine metabolite levels in excess of 100 times normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: These levels were diagnostic of pheochromocytoma.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We conclude that certain aspects of Plaintiff’s claims do not require expert testimony.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We affirm this ruling of the district court.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Richter was discharged from PHS at 2 p.m. on April 23, 2001.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Her gastroenterologist referred Mrs. Richter to Dr. Lovato for surgery to remove the tumor.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Richter died while under the care of Drs. Lovato and Winterkorn due to complications that arose when she developed an arrhythmia during the embolectomy procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It was not error for the district court to grant a partial directed verdict in favor of Dr. Winterkorn and we affirm.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We draw from these cases two principles. First, challenges to the timing of the delivery of laboratory reports will normally require expert testimony.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: The district court applied a variant of this analysis when it ruled that TriCore’s acts or failures to act in delivering the Lab Results were not medical but, rather, were “ministerial” in nature.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: We remand for further proceedings against TriCore and PHS consistent with this Opinion.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs. Richter was admitted to PHS in April 2001 for treatment and testing related to cardiac symptoms.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

requestables.name: Urine Catecholamine and Metanephrine Testing
requestables.description: Testing to determine if pheochromocytoma is present

reveal.result_summary: Catecholamine levels 150 times higher than normal; Metanephrine levels 100 times higher than normal
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt immediate follow-up and management for pheochromocytoma

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation

Lab results received but not acted upon

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient admitted with cardiac symptoms, lab tests ordered
decision_points.prompt: What should be done with the lab results indicating pheochromocytoma?

options.description: Immediately inform the treating physician and initiate management for pheochromocytoma
options.clinical_reasoning: Timely management of pheochromocytoma is critical to prevent complications

options.description: Do not act on the lab results
options.clinical_reasoning: Failure to act on critical lab results

actual_action_defendant.description: The lab results were not acted upon by the physicians

expected_action_court.description: The lab results should have been acted upon immediately
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established that the lab results were diagnostic and required immediate action

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to act on diagnostic lab results led to undiagnosed pheochromocytoma
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate management for pheochromocytoma should have been initiated
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed a heart arrhythmia during surgery and died

key_findings: Failure to act on diagnostic test results
Comparative negligence considered for non-party physicians

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to act on elevated catecholamine levels
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Appropriate management could have prevented fatal outcome
